content.problemStatement:
Take the sides of a triangle as user inputs and find if the triangle is equilateral, isosceles, or scalene.

**Note:** 

Equilateral Triangle: If all three sides of the triangle are equal, it is an equilateral triangle.

Isosceles Triangle: If at least two sides of the triangle are equal, it is an isosceles triangle.

Scalene Triangle: If all three sides of the triangle are different, it is a scalene triangle.


Input Format

The only line of input will contain three space separated numbers - The sides of a triangle.


Output Format

Output on a single line:

**Equilateral**, if the triangle is equilateral.
**Isosceles**, if the triangle is isosceles.
**Scalene**, if the triangle is scalene.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains three space‑separated integers a, b, c representing the sides of a triangle.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a separate line one word: Equilateral, Isosceles, or Scalene, according to the type of the triangle.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: sides 2,3,2 have two equal sides → Isosceles.\nTest case 2: 3,4,5 are all different → Scalene.\nTest case 3: 4,4,4 are all equal → Equilateral.
First triangle 5,5,8 has two equal sides → Isosceles.\nSecond triangle 7,9,10 has all sides different → Scalene.
All three sides are 1, so the triangle is Equilateral.

solution.approach: The type of a triangle depends only on equality of its side lengths. For each test case we compare the three sides: if all three are equal the triangle is Equilateral; else if any two are equal it is Isosceles; otherwise all sides differ and it is Scalene. The triangle inequality is guaranteed by the constraints, so no extra validation is needed. This check is O(1) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) time – a constant amount of work per test case.
